result:
The most interesting thing about silence is that it’s never truly silent. I’m sitting here now, in a room without music or voices, and the world is humming. There’s the refrigerator whispering its mechanical lullaby from the kitchen, the distant sigh of a car passing on wet pavement, the soft scratch of my own pen moving across this page. Even inside my head, it’s a cacophony—half-formed thoughts bouncing around like ping-pong balls, memories surfacing unprompted, a snippet of some song I heard yesterday stuck on an endless loop. True silence is a myth we chase, and maybe that’s for the best.

I’ve been thinking about how strange it is that we spend so much of our lives trying to drown out this inner noise with outer noise—podcasts in our ears during every walk, television murmuring in the background of every evening. We’re terrified of being left alone with our own minds. I wonder what we’re afraid we might hear if we let the quiet settle in. Maybe just the uncomfortable fact that we’re all really quite lonely, even when we’re surrounded by people. Not in a sad way, necessarily, but in a fundamental, existential way. We’re each trapped inside our own skulls, narrating a story no one else will ever fully hear. And sometimes, sitting here with the refrigerator’s hum and the traffic and the pen on the page, that feels less like a prison and more like a strange kind of freedom.
